topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, searing the edges of my vision. I stood by the entrance, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white moons against my skin. The canvases, my vibrant explosions of feeling, hung on the walls, a silent, mocking testament to my absence. A hush fell over the room, heavy and suffocating, punctuated only by the soft rustle of expensive fabric. I watched a woman glide past, her smile bright and effortless, completely unaware of the hollow space where my presence should have been. A tremor started low in my stomach, a familiar, sickening lurch. I wanted to dissolve into the polished floor, to simply cease existing in this brightly lit cage. Every brushstroke I had poured onto linen now felt like a betrayal, exposed and judged by strangers.

[story 2]

I traced the outline of a crimson swirl on a nearby piece, the texture of the paint cool and unforgiving beneath my fingertips. The silence in the studio was amplified, a vast, echoing chamber where my own breathing sounded deafeningly loud. When I finally dared to look toward the main hall, the sight of my work, framed and cataloged under sterile spotlights, sent a wave of nausea through me. My shoulders slumped, a sudden, overwhelming weight pressing down on my chest, making each breath a conscious, laborious effort. "It's... very bold," a voice drifted from across the room, too cheerful, too distant. I turned away quickly, my gaze fixed on a smudge on the floor, wishing the concrete beneath my worn boots would simply swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The air in the exhibition space was too thin, lacking the familiar, earthy scent of turpentine and linseed oil that usually anchored me. I shuffled toward a corner, my movements slow and heavy, like wading through thick syrup. Seeing the collection—my rawest expressions displayed as commodities—triggered a sharp, stinging sensation behind my eyes. I leaned against a cold marble pillar, trying to anchor myself, but my legs felt unsteady, refusing to cooperate. A profound emptiness settled in my core, a deep, internal chill that no amount of gallery warmth could dispel. I stared at my reflection in a darkened glass case, recognizing a stranger whose eyes held only a muted resignation. I felt utterly unmoored, a ghost haunting the periphery of my own creation.
